summary: DHH argues that immortality is a curse, not a gift, and that the finitude of life is what gives it beauty and meaning. Drawing on cultural touchstones like Highlander, Queen, and Björk, he makes the case that satiation applies to life itself just as it does to individual pleasures. He reflects on passing the halfway point of his own life expectancy and finds comfort rather than dread in eventual endings — of racing, coding, and life itself. He concludes with the Stoic idea of holding life with a loose grip, embracing both fate and death.
key_points: People intuitively understand satiation for individual pleasures but irrationally resist applying the same logic to life itself
Immortality brings loneliness, repetition, and cynicism — the curse of forever is worse than the mercy of finality
Even novelty eventually becomes variation on existing themes; surprise itself succumbs to familiarity
The knowledge that specific activities will end — racing, coding, writing — adds meaning rather than diminishing it
Holding life with a loose grip, like drumsticks, lets the music flow better until the song naturally ends
money_quotes: Immortality always sounded like a curse to me.

It's funny, people seem to have no problem understanding satiation when it comes to the individual parts of life. Enough delicious cake, no more rides on the rollercoaster, the end of a great party. But not life itself.
The eventual end strikes me as beautiful relief. Framing the idea that you can see enough, do enough, be enough.
It nails the sadness of forever. The loneliness, the repetition, the inevitable cynicism with humanity.
It's a great big world, but eventually every unseen element will appear as but a variation on an existing theme. Even surprise itself will succumb to familiarity.
One day I will write the last line of Ruby code, too. Sell the last subscription. Write the last tweet. How merciful.
One way to think of this is to hold life with a loose grip. Like a pair of drumsticks.
key_insight: Life's finitude is not a tragedy to be overcome but a mercy that gives meaning to every experience, and the wisest response is to hold it all with a loose grip.
